ranked.transcript: (relaxed music) - 'Kay, so Sony is back with their new flagship wireless noise-canceling headphones with the super easy to remember name. You know the ones. These are the Sony WH-1000XM6s. Easier way to think of it, most people just call these the XM6 or the Mark 6s, which is exciting because the Mark 5s from last year actually left some room for improvement. They improved some things, but they also made some things worse, which was weird because the Sony noise-canceling headphones were pretty much king of the hill for so long, they didn't have a ton of reason to change a whole lot. Thankfully though, the newest generation updates a lot of the stuff that the Mark 5s fell short on, so that's really good to see. It's exciting that there is a new design, new sound, a new case, but also a new price. We'll get there. So, straight from the top, I still love this super clean, simple design. They kept what was good about the Mark 5s. They are very, very lightweight. The vegan leather earpads are super soft. The headband is incredibly flexible. These are just some of the most comfortable headphones to wear for a long period of time, which is what you want from these. If you wear them for a long time for traveling or for a commute or something like that, they are still awesome. But if you remember the last generation, maybe you're a subscriber and you've watched all these videos, wink, wink, then you remember that they made this huge downgrade to travel specifically because they didn't fold at all, ever, so they had this huge case that was more annoying to travel with. So, first thing I was happy to see is the Mark 6s are back to being able to fold up again, so they have a new, significantly smaller case designed around them that fits some cables as well, and bonus, the case doesn't zipper anymore. It just closes with magnets at this buckle on the side and it is way faster to open and close than everyone else's zipper cases. I'm actually not sure how we haven't seen this more often because this case is pretty much perfect. I have a feeling it's about to be copied a whole lot in a whole bunch of places, 'cause wouldn't change a thing. Also, another thing that Mark 5s weirdly didn't do was when you're wearing 'em around your neck was just to be able to rotate them flat like this. For some reason, they took that away in the Mark 5s, and now they're back. Simple thing, but thank you. So, overall design-wise, I feel like these are back to being up near the top of the best travel-ready noise canceling headphones. It also comes with a four-foot right-angle headphone cable, and one of those tiny USB-C to USB-A cables for charging and they just fit in this little pocket over here in the corner of the case like this. It's great. Feels durable. No notes. And then they changed the buttons and microphones around the outside of the headphones just slightly, so the right side is still just the USB Type-C port, and then on the left next to the audio jack, they've made the power button much bigger, which doesn't seem like much of a big deal, but that now lets you distinguish better between the power button and the noise cancellation button while you're not able to see them. Now, speaking of noise cancellation, they made a bunch of behind-the-scenes changes to these headphones that sound like they could make a big improvement. So, there are 12 microphones on these instead of eight from the last generation, there's also a significantly more powerful Sony QN3 chip inside that should be able to do much more processing, and they also said that they've trained these headphones on 500 million voice samples using AI to be able to better distinguish human voices from environmental noise. So, this is the last generation of headphones and how the built in microphone sounds to someone I'm on a call with. Eight microphones. You know, some noise from air conditioners and general room tones, but nothing too crazy. And so now here is the Mark 6. So, they added two more beamforming microphones to the front for hearing my voice better, but what I really think most of these microphones are better for is noise cancellation and transparency. So, how do they sound? Well, I've been listening to these headphones for the past two weeks or so. I've taken a few flights, I've worn 'em around the studio, at home, all kinds of different environments, and they sound better. These are actually all-new drivers versus the last gen, so there's a lot going on here, but to be specific, they are excellent quality sound with great range. Punchy lows and a lot of bass and then a lot of detail and clarity all the way through the mids and the highs and they are still really, really insanely good at canceling out white noise. Now, if we wanna get even more granular, I did a bunch of back-to-back listening with these Mark 6s and the last generation, and there are certain tracks where you really feel the difference a lot. Basically, the Mark 6s have a much stronger low end, especially in the sub-bass and lowest bass, and the Mark 5s started to feel a little spiky in the midrange where the vocals are and were a little bit weaker in the low end, so in the tracks that have a really important bass or sub-bass, pop, electro, hip hop, stuff like that, that's when in these, they're really appreciated. And then the Mark 6 also has even stronger noise cancellation than the already GOATed Mark 5s, but the real highlight for me is actually the transparency mode, which feels way more natural and clear sounding. So, if you're wearing these headphones and do want voices to come through or just wanna hear your environment more for whatever reason, people might talk to you through these headphones, the transparency mode, or what Sony calls ambient sound mode, went from feeling a little muddy and unclear to literally up there with AirPods Max levels of crystal clear and natural, which is awesome. Now, not only are these headphones new, but Sony's also refreshed their app. The Sony Headphones Connect app is now just called Sound Connect, but it still does most of the same stuff. There's still literally 20 different levels of ambient sound. There's still custom EQs that you can easily flip between, and this is also where you can turn on or off any of the other convenience features. Now, this one new one that I found interesting is listening mode. So, you can listen to your audio like normal in standard mode, or you can flip it to background music mode, which does an incredibly good job of making it sound like the music is coming from a small set of tinny speakers, like, 20 feet away, like, as if you're in a cafe somewhere, which, you know, you might be into that. There's even actually three modes. So, cafe, living room, or my room, each of which just shrinks the size of the virtual room a bit, and then there's also cinema mode, which does this upmixing from stereo to a 360-degree immersive sound mix of whatever you're listening to, kinda like a movie theater. It's also a very dramatic V-shaped sound too. I could picture someone maybe in their home office or a cubicle or something like that listening to these all day and wanting to feel like it's a less fatiguing, further away sound, and then you'd put cafe mode on and it works. It's surprising how well it works. And maybe then you flip to cinema mode for watching certain YouTube videos or something like that. There's also still the EQ stuff. The point is you can make these headphones sound exactly the way you want them to. The sound is really strong. The noise cancellation is excellent. They're extremely comfortable to wear for long periods of time. They fold up into a smaller case. The battery life is the same 30 hours as last year. My only downsides are literally that your ears can get a little sweaty without any ventilation after a couple hours and the touch pad controls on the side are still finicky like they are every year, but clearly these have a lot of great things going for them. So then the price. $449. These are even more pricey now, and the Mark 5s were already super premium at 400 bucks when they launched, and now they've dropped down into the $350 range. These are launching at $450, maybe partially to account for tariffs, maybe partially because they think they're that much better now, but that is really expensive, and I was thinking about it for a while, and I actually still think they are worth it, and I basically landed on that because this whole category of all these other premium noise-canceling headphones options is filled with other things that still try to beat these and don't and make these still feel like a deal, if that makes sense. Also, especially for Android people. Like, the Bose QuietComfort Ultras are $429. These are better. The Sonos headphones launched at $450 and have dropped a little bit, but these are better. JBL has a $400 option. This absolutely smokes those. This smokes the Beat Studios. AirPods Max are still somehow $550. You could argue though for the Apple ecosystem people that maybe that's worth it, but these Sonys still have multi-device connectivity and can combine an Apple device and a non-Apple device, which is sick. So, my take is if you're still just looking for the flat out best headphones to fly with, to work with, to commute with, that you gotta have the most comfort, the best noise cancellation, I mean, that's just how much they cost. These are back on the throne. So, there you have it. Sony's WH-1000XM6. Thanks for watching. Catch you in the next one. Peace. (relaxed music)

comments: A review of a noise-cancelling headphone without noise-cancelling review.
Makes a 10-minute long video about a premium set of noise canceling headphones... never mentions noise canceling...
I trust comments more than this reviewing guy.

I learned a lot from this: No Lossless via USB-C, Dolby Atmos has to be tweak first in iOS setting to get always on, but still no head tracking, and the ear that touch the inner headphone is uncomfortable.

I just grabbed the XM6 and now I have XM4 to compare to side by side. Foreshadowing, I have a BIG head, I max out all my headphones, including the Sennheiser 6XX. They sound different, I EQ-ed them both so not a hard comparison, but the XM6 is more precise and ever so more stable. Overall, the XM6 is an upgrade on all charts, except comfort. 1) they clamp harder, 2) the headband is making me reposition them back and forth slightly every one or two hours, 3) the maxed out sliders mean that they will "twist" more easily - imagine your headphones sitting symmetrically on your left and right ears, but these can twist (rotating in opposite directions), I would not immediately notice, but it can cause assymetric pressure (and thus wear/pain if I clamp my earlobe/helix) AND can cause noise/pressure leaks that do make L/R channels ever so different (and so infuriating after I started paying attention to this). To be fair, for 3) I now physically make sure they are rotated symmetrically and I have not noticed an issue during normal wearing where they would mispossition themselves. Also a small 4) - the new mic array and processor for ANC are doing a killer job at killing the more annoying high-pitched noise, I think the overall step up is by 2dB across the spectrum, but do a better job in the London underground and in coffee shops, ever so slightly. XM4 are already awesome in this regard, this is marginal and not a reason to upgrade!

I also had the opportunity to try out the Bose QuietComfort Ultra 2nd Gen, just for an hour or so. First impression was that they do a better job at soundstage out of the box and the earcup/headband comfort is better. How the headband comfort would project into a couple hours I do not know. But they hide their weight better certainly. I was not able to A/B them yet. But I am contemplating if the sound/ANC is worth the comfort compared to the XM6.

Thanks for the update, I still have my XM4 and won't be changing. I bought XM5 and instantly sent them back, they were an awful update, I've been watching the XM6, but I'm happy with the 4's I have and they have lasted me years now, with only changing the ear pads once so far.

ranked.transcript: I've had the Sony WH-1000XM6 headphones for six months now, and I'm going to walk you through the standout features, the things I can't stand about them, and whether I think you should pick up a pair. First, let's talk about sound quality. This is the main reason I think you would go with these headphones. They have great clarity, and it's very tight around your ears audio-wise. It feels like the instruments are just right outside of your ears, probably thanks to their driver placement and shallower ear cups compared to other headphones. Though, I do have an issue with that driver placement, which I'll talk about in the downsides portion of this review. The sound is well-balanced. Nothing feels like it has too much bass or treble. It's not as punchy by default as something like the Bose QuietComfort Ultra Gen 2 that I'm reviewing, but you still get a lot of great dynamic sound with these, and they're one of, if not the best headphones that I've tested where the sound profile is this good out of the box. Next up, let's talk about the design. I went with the blue color, and it just looks stunning in person. It still provides the darker contrast you may want while wearing them, but without appearing totally black. The folding hinges make the case significantly smaller than those of many competitors, which is a huge plus for portability. Another great thing about the case is that instead of a zipper, it uses this magnetic clasp, which has worked really well over the past six months, and I much prefer it over a zipper. The headband also looks more premium with fewer seams that you can see compared to the previous generation. They're also extremely lightweight at 254 grams compared to the other headphones that they're competing against, like the AirPods Max and Nothing Headphone 1, which I'm currently reviewing. And make sure you subscribe to the channel if you want to see my thoughts on those headphones. Another feature I really appreciate is the guided EQ feature. It helps you find your own EQ setting by allowing you to start playing music and then try different options to determine what sounds better. But by default, I actually found these just sound great on their own, so I just kept them with their default tuning. The next standout feature is battery life. These have great battery life at 30 hours. Now, that's comparable to most of the headphones that these compete with, and they beat out the AirPods Max by a full 10 hours. Next, let's talk about mic quality and transparency mode, which are both highlights. The transparency mode on these when talking to other people is pretty damn good. Now, the Sony headphones have the best mics out of any that I've tested. And here's an example of how they sound. All right. And these are the Sony's. This is how their mics sound. And when I'm wearing these, I can definitely—I can hear a little bit of my voice. The only thing I don't like is how much Sony amplifies your voice when you're using the mics on these. It just makes things sound a bit unnatural to me compared to what other headphones do. And there doesn't seem to be a way to tone that down like you get with the Nothing Headphone 1 as well as other headphones via their app. Fortunately, you can thankfully turn this side feature off in Sony's headphones app. The transparency mode is also quite good on these and sounds, I'd say just—it's a step below the AirPods Max, which still have the best transparency mode out of any pairs of headphones I've used. And with those, it just sounds like you're not even wearing headphones. With these, it's pretty close to that, but you can still tell when you hear your own voice. That's the real tell that it's like, "No, I'm definitely still wearing headphones." So, what features have I not found myself using with these headphones? All the extra features in the Sony app, such as contextual listening modes and ambient sound control, which adjusts the amount of noise cancellation based on your environment. I didn't really use these. I do like that you can back up your headphones settings to Sony's cloud, but features like automatic switching from headphones to Sony's speakers and services like Amazon Music Play Now or Endel, I didn't use those either. 360 Reality Audio is another feature that you'll see in Sony's app. And this technology is basically Sony's rival to Dolby Atmos. Now, outside of Amazon Music, Tidal actually dropped support for this format back in 2024. It's not really a feature that's worth it in my opinion. Sony—they may have won the Blu-ray battle way back when, but I think it's pretty clear at this point they've lost the format wars to Atmos. Now, these headphones can do Atmos. I just for whatever reason have found the AirPods Max with Apple Music—that just seems to be the combination where Atmos music has sounded the best to me on a pair of headphones for whatever reason. All right, now let's talk about some of the downsides I've encountered with these headphones. First up is comfort. By making the ear cups more shallow and placing the drivers closer to your ears, it actually makes wearing these headphones way less comfortable than their competition. I feel like the inner part of the ear cup just sits on and puts too much pressure specifically on my right ear, which makes it really hard for me to wear these for any extended period of time. And this is not something that I've experienced with any of the other major wireless headphones that I've reviewed. This was something that I noticed with the previous Sony headphones that I had hoped would have been fixed with this version, but it was not. Another downside is the controls. You basically have to turn these headphones off and then back on again just to get them into Bluetooth pairing mode, which is annoying. And I don't care for the touch controls either. While they do work, I'd much prefer something more physical and precise like the digital crown on the AirPods Max or the roller button on the Nothing Headphone 1. Basically, I think Sony has gone almost too minimalist here with their two physical buttons. And while I do appreciate the lightweight construction, I will say they don't feel as premium with the materials they use compared to the nice metal Bose is using in their new design or the aluminum material used in the Nothing Headphone 1 and AirPods Max. Another downside is aesthetics when wearing these headphones. They just stick out from your head a bit more than competing headphones do, which I think makes them look a bit more dorky and less sleek than some of their competition. Another downside is there's no lossless audio via USB-C. This feels like an odd miss for a company that cares so much about great sound quality. Now, almost all of the competing wireless headphones in this space now have this feature, so it's a bit of an odd miss. There's also no Apple Music Quick Trigger. Meta Ray-Bans have this, so it is a thing that exists, and I'm not sure why Sony didn't implement it in their app. Now, one slightly innovative thing Sony added to these headphones was their own assistant. You just say, "Hey, headphones," and you can control playback or bring up your phone's assistant. Now, this is pretty cool in theory because the commands and the way to trigger them will stay consistent regardless of the device you're using to listen to music. The only issue I've had with this feature is it really hasn't worked very consistently for me over the past six months. Like I constantly get the "I'm sorry I couldn't recognize that command" error when I say "Hey headphones, pause music" or "volume up." You have to be even more precise than your typical smart assistant with the command that you want the headphones to execute, which is a bit annoying. So do I recommend getting the Sony WH-1000XM6 headphones? In general, yes, especially if you can find them on sale and if they fit your ears okay. And be sure to check out the product link in this video as well as the one that I post in the description and pinned comment where you can see the current prices of all of the products I talked about in this video. Links to multiple retailers for price comparison shopping and you can click that eye icon at the top right-hand corner of the product cards on our website to see where else we've covered that product. Definitely buy them from a retailer with a decent return policy. For me, if I weren't reviewing these, I probably would have returned them just because I can't stand their fit. But I know many people can wear them without issue and everyone's ears and head shape is different. So, if you can, they do sound fantastic. They have excellent noise cancellation and are pretty lightweight. Those are honestly the main reasons to go for them. If you're an Apple user, however, yes, I do think it's worth taking a look at the AirPods Max for their Apple ecosystem integration features, especially if you can find them on sale during the holidays. 우리가 통화를 하고 있다가 아니면 미팅을 하고 있다가 만약 음속으로 해야 될 상황이 있잖아요. 그러면 핸드폰에서 음속으로 눌러야 했는데 마크 6에서는 노이즈 캔슬링 버튼을 두 번 클릭하면 자체적으로 마이크를 끌 수 있는 기능도 새롭게 추가됐습니다. 핸드폰으로 조작 안 하고 핸드폰 자체에서 조절할 수 있다 보니까이 음성을 기능도 되게 좋았어요. 또 LC3 코덱이 새롭게 추가됐습니다. 요거는 제가 예전에 콘텐츠로 몇 번 이야기를 했었거든요. 이거는 오디오에서 새롭게 주관된 코덱이에요. 특징이라면 일반적으로 많이 쓰이는 SBC 코덱보다 압축률도 좋고 특히 레이턴시가 짧기 때문에 리듬 게임 같이 딜레이에 민감하신 분들에게 되게 좋은 코덱기 LC3 고입니다. 그래서 이제 소니 앱에서도 클래식 오디오 그까 SBC나 L덱 같은 거 이제 연결을 할 것이냐 오디오로 기본적으로 우선으로 연결할 것이냐 이렇게 선택 가능합니다. 그리고 소소하게 계산된 부분들도 좀 있더라고요. 자, 먼저 이게 기존까지는 이게 왜 안 됐을까 싶었던 기능. 바크 6에선 충전하면서 음악을들을 수 있도록 개선됐습니다. 전작은 충전하면서 노래를들을 수 없었어요. 근데 또 아쉬운 점요 헤드폰이 3.5파이 이어폰잭을 지원한단 말이에요. 그리고 C 타입도 충전을 위해서 있는데 요것도 이제 C 타입으로 오디오 출력까지 가능했으면 충전과 동시에 오디오 출력이 되다 보니까 그 점에 있어서도 더 좋지 않았을까 하는 아쉬움이 낫기도 해요. 그리고 청취 모드에서 영화관이라는게 또 추가됐거든요. 이게 처음에들을 때는 그냥 그랬는데요. 이제 어떨지 한번 들어봅시다. 요것도 확실히 공강함이 살긴 한데 그 인위적으로 공강함을 만들어 주다 보니까 좀 어색함은 있습니다. 그래서 영화 트레일러를 시청해 봤거든요. 음악보다는 확실히 자연스럽고 신기하더라고요. 하지만 원본 소스가 2D인 걸 3D로 만들었다 보니까 약간은 늘려 버린듯한 느낌이 들어서 좀 어색한 느낌이 들긴 해요. 그리고 마지막으로 배터리. 제가 써본 노이즈 캔슬링 헤드폰 중에서는 배틀리가 제일 오래 봐요. 출퇴근할 때 쓰잖아요. 거의 충전을 뭐 일주에 한 번 해도 엄청 남을 정도로 배터리 타임은 아마 소니가 거의 최강자이지 않을까 싶습니다. 소니에서 무려 3년 만에 새롭게 등장한만큼 전작에서 지작된 단점들을 상당히 많이 개선해서 새로운 마크 6로 출시했습니다. 특히 이제 저식 헤드폰으로 돌아온게 되게 많이 좋았고요. 체감되는 건 호불러 없이 음질 그리고 노이즈 캔슬링 성능 부분에서는 많은 분들께서 업그레이드 되었다고 느끼실 거예요. 다만 이제 가격도 조금 높다는 점도 아쉽죠. 단순 환율로 봤을 땐 한국이 전 세계 중에서는 거의 싸게 나오는 편이라고 해요. 유독 저렴하게 출시한 일본을 제외하곤 국내가 가장 저렴하지만 기본적인 가격 자체가 좀 많이 오르다 보니까 되게 많이 아쉬워진 것이죠. 저는이 정도 성능의이 정도 개선점이라면 납득할 정도의 딱 거의 마지노선이지 않을까? 만약 새로운 버전이 나왔을 때 이거보다 가격이 더 올라간다 그러면 선택지가 더 많이 다행해질 것 같습니다. 마크 4에서 마크 5로 왔을 때 좀 애매하다고 느껴졌었는데 마크 6는 확실한 업그레이드처럼 느껴졌습니다. 아무튼 오늘 여기까지며 만약 어 이렇게 마크 5랑 마크 6랑 이제 선택지가 있어요. 둘쪽 머살레라고 한다면 저는 마크 6로 살 것 같아요. 기능적으로도 마크 6가 많이 좋아졌고 사실 마크 5는 디자인이 많이 바뀌면서 약간 가격 대비 좀 고급감이 없었거든요. 근데 마크 6는 그러한 부분에서 많이 채워져 갖고 오랜만에 또 재밌는 노이즈 캔슬링 헤드폰이 나온 거

샌드스톤 색상 같은 경우는 따뜻하면서도 고급스러운 색감을 가지고 있었고요. 그렇기 때문에 어떤 상황에도 잘 어울리는 컬러였습니다. 특히 오해 사용해도 질리지 않는 컬러라는 점이 특징이라고 할 수 있었습니다. 새로운 색상이 추가된 것이기 때문에 사운드나 노이즈 캔슬링 성능은 기존에 출시된 다른 색상을 통해서 잘 알려진 것처럼 좋은 성능과 사운드를 가지고 있습니다. 최신 HD 노이즈 캔슬링 프로세서인 QN3와 12개의 마이크가 탑재가 되어 있어서 전작 대비 노이즈 캔슬링 성능이 더 좋아졌는데요. 그렇기 때문에 대중 교통을 이용할 때 카페 등에서 조용하게 영상과 음악을 즐길 수 있습니다. 제가 외부에서 이렇게 차가 지나가는 상황에서도 직접 테스트를 해 봤는데 확실히 노이즈가 제거되면서이 컨텐츠에 집성할 수 있더라고요. 이러한 노이즈 캔슬링 성능은 헤드폰이기 때문에 이어폰에 비해서 더 좋은 성능을 여러분들도 착용하시면 바로 느끼실 수 있을 것 같아요. 그러면서이 제품이 인기를 얻고 있는 이유는 바로 좋은 사운드 대문이라고 할 수 있죠. 크레미어드에서 수상을 했거나 또는 노미네이트된 마스터 엔지니어들과 협업해서 만든 사운드는 LDAC도 지원하기 때문에 하이 레졸루션 오디오를 감상할 수 있습니다. 확실히 음악들을 들어보시면이 제품이 가지고 있는 매력을 여러분들도 쉽게 느끼실 수 있을 것 같아요. 개인적으로는 과하지 않으면서 해상력이 좋은 사운드를들을 수 있다는 점이 바로 사운드적으로이 제품이 가지고 있는 매력인 거 같습니다. 그리고 소니 헤드폰 같은 경우는 사운드 커넥트 앱을 이용해서 다양한 기능을 설정할 수 있고요. 영화, 게임 등 콘텐츠 맞춤형으로 변경해서 사용하실 수 있으세요. 자, 보시는 것처럼 이렇게 주변 소리 제어 기능도 여기서 이렇게 노이즈 캔슬링, 주변 사운드 등으로 설정을 하실 수 있고요. 이퀄라이즈도 참 이런 식으로 뭐 게임을 선택하실 수 있고 명확함, 무거움, 수동 이런 식으로 선택해서 적용을 하실 수 있습니다. 그리고 아래쪽을 이렇게 내리게 되면 배경 음악 설정이 가능하고요. 그리고 영화관으로 설정을 하실 수도 있고 그리고 적응형 사운드 제어 기능도 여러분들이 설정해서 사용하실 수 있으세요. 저 같은 경우도 요즘 영화를 볼 때 집에서도 이제 가족들이 잘 때 연결해서 사용하고 있기도 하고 게임을 할 때도 사용하고 있는데요. 확실히 앱을 통해서 최적화 모드를 설정을 해 주면 그냥 일반 모드로 음악 모드로들을 때하고는 확실히 다른 느낌으로 감상할 수 있더라고요. 그렇기 때문에이 제품을 앞으로 만나 보시는 분들 또는 현재 사용하고 있는 분들은 꼭 콘텐츠에 맞춰서 사용을 하시는 걸 추천해 드리고 싶습니다. 마지막으로 헤드폰 같은 경우는 착용감도 상당히 중요한 포인트죠. 어, 장시간 착용할 수 있는 편안한 착용감을 제공하냐.이 이 부분이 가장 중요한 포인트 중에 하나라고 할 수 있는데 일단 기본적으로이 모델 방향 표시가 잘 되어 있기 때문에 방향 표시를 보고 착용을 해 주시면 되고요. 이어컵의 쿠션이 좋고요. 헤어밴드도 넓고 두껍기 때문에 장시간 착용을 할 때도 편하게 착용할 수 있었습니다. 예전에는 헤드폰 하면 겨울이라고 생각하시는 분들이 많았지만 요즘은이 헤드폰이 패션 아이템이 되면서 여름에도 착용을 하시는 분들이 많은데요. 이번에 출시된 샌드스톤 색상 같은 경우는 여름에 사용하기에도 좋은 컬러고 그리고 다양한 의상이나 상황에 맞춤형으로 잘 어울리는 색상인 것 같습니다. 그렇기 때문에 소니 WH1x 마크 6 모델을 선택하려고 하시는 분들은 다른 제품들과 차별적인 샌드스톤 색상을 만나 보시면 어떨까 싶습니다. 새롭게 색상이 출시가 되었기 때문에 어떤 느낌일지 궁금하신 분들을 위해서 다른 색상들과 함께 비교를 해 봤는데요. 궁금하셨던 분들의 궁금증이 해결되는 리뷰였으면 좋겠네요.

4:30 I completely disagree. USB-C audio is not a step forward, and the industry doesn’t always dictate things correctly. In fact, having a 3.5mm jack is a competitive advantage compared to your list of "competitors" — and one I welcome. It operates entirely passively, without requiring power. You can always buy an external DAC for your active devices (which can’t connect to 3.5mm), but the reverse doesn’t work, because DACs need power. ranked.transcript: It's finally happened. Sony released the WH100XM 6, one of the most hyped headphone releases of the year. And you probably want to know, did they fix the mistakes of headphones past? Yes. Yes. Yes. Sony definitely took getting roasted over the WH100XM 5 to heart, and it did an admirable job of squashing bugs. But while the old bug bears of the previous models are largely addressed, there are new problems that come up. Problems that will make you think twice about buying the new XM6. But let me explain. The new Sony WH100XM6 looks quite a bit like the older WH100XM5, but this time it has redesigned hinges with a metal skeleton to avoid breakage, deeper rear cups, redesigned driver elements, upgraded A&C, and a vastly improved processor to handle digital signal processing for the sound and noise cancelling. Under the hood, the WH100XM6 has a 30 mm driver with an ANC mic in front of each. On the bottom of the left ear cup is an analog jack that you can plug a male to-male TRRS cable into for wired listening. And the new snapping soft shell case has a pocket to stow your easily lost wires. A number of other creature comforts were added like being able to listen while you charge your headphones, a larger power button that's impossible to miss while the cans are on your head, and an absurd amount of microphones for noise cancellation. 12 in total, six of which are used for phone calls, but more on ANC later. While this is all great on paper, and it is, it is, what does any of this mean for you, what about comfort or sound quality or any of the things that really matter with headphones? That's a little bit more complicated. For starters, while the design has undoubtedly been improved, a couple of new foibless have cropped up. The first is comfort. The wider headband accommodates more noggin than before, but the relatively thin padding remains a sore spot. Even brand new, the plastic chassis sits very close to flush on my head. So, I'm worried about what happens when the visco elastic foam starts to lose its original shape and gets thinner over time. Especially annoying for those of you with glasses. It also may impact active noise cancellation performance as we saw with the XM5. So, it's worth noting that these headphones might run into the same issue. Additionally, that ANC assembly in front of the speaker driver is quite large and protrudes a couple millimeters from the plane of the mesh. Because of this, if you have smaller ears, accidentally put the headphones on backwards, or generally just get an odd fit with the WH100 XM6, it could touch your pinup and rub the mesh on your ear over time. Not many people will have this problem, but if you do find yourself chafing a little, just try receding the headphones a little bit further back. Sony also threw in everything but the kitchen sink here, as it like everyone else has bet big on features like spatial audio, sound virtualization modes, ambient pass through modes, and more. Additionally, the headphones are able to support spatial audio upmixing, which takes each channel and processes it to what the headphones think is needed to make a stereo recording a spatial one. I prefer a regular old stereo mix, but if you're a spatial audio fan, it's worth seeing if you like it. I don't, but it's not something that would stop me from buying headphones if I can get away with never using it, and I won't. The Bluetooth stack is also upgraded to 5.3, not the latest 5.4. And you can listen over SBC, AAC, LC3, and LDAC. Lie audio is also able to be toggled in the app, as well as virtualization modes, 10B EQ, and audio quality selection. Sony's latest release claims the headphones are compatible with OraCast, though we haven't gotten that to work here in the office just yet. I didn't have any issues here with LDAC, but you might be interested to know that there are some compatibility issues with some Android phones that can make the full 990 kilobits per second transfer rate a little unstable. So, choosing the 660 kilobit per second option will be much less annoying while being free from issues. iPhone users will be locked to AAC, but that's hardly an issue nowadays. And I mean, what's new? It's Apple. GEC. I'm also a little miffed where Apple sure Sennheiser Jab JBL Focal Bowers and Wilkins and others have made USBC audio standard on noise cancelling headphones. The WH100XM6 does not have this capability. Instead, the 3.5 mm analog jack is present for wired listening. I see this as a huge missed opportunity as it seems like the industry has decided that USB is the next logical step in wired audio and it really sucks not to be able to have a truly digital lossless option where the competition does. All right, so how's the battery? In our standardized battery test, the Sony WH100XM 6 was able to play back music peaking at 75 dBpl. While this is a rock solid result, the top tier of A&C headphones is a bit more competitive than you might think. For while these cans absolutely mop the floor with Apple AirPods Max at 20 hours and the Bose Quiet Comfort Ultra at 27 hours and 37 minutes, they fall short of the Sennheiser Momentum 4 Wireless at 56 hours and the JBL Tour 1M3 at 55 plus hours. The battery of the Sony WH100XM6 is just enough to cover an entire work week. If you take your legally mandated breaks or almost four weeks of commutes if you have an hour to travel each way like me, it sucks. If you want to top up on juice, a 3minut charge should net you about 3 hours worth of listening time. I mean, headphones don't have massive batteries to begin with, so we don't really need any wildly fast charging to satisfy your needs here. With a six microphone array with a processor that can make use of an AI analysis of heaps of recordings of, you know, what not to pass to a phone, the Sony WH100XM 6 is excellent at rejecting unwanted noise, especially wind noise, as you can hear for yourself on the soundguys.com website. Go to the full review or up there or other places. Just click things. While you're not going to record the next top 100 hit on these headphones, and if you do, post proof in the comments. We'll eat our words. The array is more than good enough to handle business calls, personal calls, really just any phony conversations you want without dropouts or excess noise. If you're looking for Sony to fight to retake the throne of best A&C headphones, it did to a point. On the back of extremely good isolation, the powerful new A&C system is able to put the WH100XM6 on the same tier shared only by the Apple AirPods Max JBL Tour 1 M3 and slightly leaprogs the Bose Quiet Comfort Ultra. If you don't want any junk noise to reach your ears, the Sony WH100XM6 is on the short list of the most effective attenuators. This is due in part to the upgraded infrastructure of both AI optimization and the new Q3 chip purported to be 7 times faster than the chip in the WH100XM5s. I found this quite good on Vancouver Sky Train. And while drowning out the neighbors untimely, absurdly messy divorce by physically blocking out a ton of noise without needing the ANC to handle it. What does break through generally isn't all that loud compared to how it could have been. But to help visualize what I mean, I've taken a standard set of noise fields and measured the difference at the eardrum between running the samples without any headphones on and with the Sony WH100XM6's ANC enabled. The audio behind the samples is not to be taken exactly what you'll hear, but a general comparison of how much noise is attenuated. The brighter the area on the chart is, the louder the sound. The darker you get it visualization. And here you go. Have a listen. If you want to let some noise in to manage your surroundings, Sony retains one of the best pass through modes in the market and allowed me to handle swapping between full blast A&C and being able to talk to the kids by cupping my ear over the right ear cup. And you can even toggle a speak to chat feature like the one found in the Google PixelBuds Pro 2 in order to have a conversation without fussing with the settings on your phone before listening to somebody. But I reiterate, don't approach people wearing headphones. They don't want to talk to you. Just stop. Get some help. Perhaps the biggest improvement to the WH100XM 6 over the WH100XM 5 is the sound quality. Though audio files are still probably going to turn up their noses of the level of bass these headphones provide, the tuning Sony gave the new flagship product is projected to satisfy more people right out of the box. Without any EQ presets, profiles, or other userdefined processing enabled, the WH100 XM6 knocked it out of the park. According to Head Acoustics multi-dimensional audio quality scores, with a 4.8 8 out of five overall and no score below a 4.3. On a scale of 1 to five, that's notably good for A&C headphones, which typically struggle a little bit, as evidenced by the WH100XM 5, which scored a 4.2. Overall, ANC headphones tend not to be the most amazing at this whole sound quality thing, and that's still true. But at least these will be wellreceived by most people, even despite the challenges of the format. Switching off to the objective metrics, we can see that the frequency response, how each frequency along the range of human hearing is emphasized, that the WH100 XM6 didn't completely start from scratch. However, the bass is emphasized more as a slope rather than a shelf that starts too high. XM5 and the highs can be a more reasonable level as a result. Speaking of, the highs are quite different than those of the WH100XM5 as the treble extension is much greater though with some strong peaks at 16 and 10.5 kHz. So, what does that mean for you? Well, probably not a ton. Though, you may end up wanting to tamp down on the 10.5 kHz peak a little bit to prevent some annoyingly loud atmospheric or room effect sounds from getting too grading. If you're older, you'll likely appreciate that 16 kHz bump as it's a bit similar to null's proposed solution to meeting listener preferences of 40 plus year olds. Hey, wait a minute. I'm listening to Prague Rock like King Gizzard and the Lizard Wizard's most recent albums will sound quite good to most of your dad rock fans. However, listeners under 25 may elect to use the Sound Connect app to touch down the uppermost octave by a few decb only to creep it back up as the inexraable march of time proceeds, bringing your old age ever closer to a crushing and undeniability of your own mortality. In any case, a response like this is quite good for immersiveness as it emphasizes the ranges of human hearing responsible for little details, room effects, and echoes that might make it into the mix. If you do want to change anything, the WH1000XM6 is a far more reasonable starting point than the older XM 5 because it is much harder to EQ away that high bass shelf that the XM6 does not have, which is the source of a lot of gripes surrounding the headphones performance. The WH100XM6 at least allows you a little less opportunity to make things sound too weird when you go tinkering, as the stock sound is pretty decent already. But if you're asking me whether you should rush out and drop $449.99 on these headphones, I'm going to have to say maybe. They're certainly at the top of the pile for 2025. And yeah, they're excellent performers. However, when you're sinking that kind of coin on something you're going to use every day, having the entire feature set that you need is going to be important. And in that regard, the WH100XM 6 is a little bit riskier of a purchase than some other competitors. I say that because while Sony did an excellent job addressing the shortcomings of previous WH100X series headphones, the company didn't look to make any big innovations or accommodations to how people are going to need headphones to work in the coming years. The WH-1000XM6 headphones come with better sound, better ANC, much needed design improvements and a higher price tag. Starting at $449 the flagship headphones also come with spatial audio upmixing, AI beam-forming for clearer voice calls, more microphones and battery life up to 40h (or 30h with ANC on). ranked.transcript: All right, it's that time again. Sony has new flagship noise cancelling headphones on the way. And I'll bet you can even guess what they're called. Yeah, the WH000XM6 are here. And so, at first glance, they're pretty similar to the M5s that came out three long years ago. And there's no real one big feature that sets these apart from the M5s either. Sure, the noise cancellation is more powerful, and Sony's put a lot of focus on sound quality this time around, but ultimately, this is a situation where a lot of small refinements and improvements add up to a must-have upgrade for some people anyway. Okay, so there's no better place to start than with their design, and Sony stuck with the same overall look and feel, but has made some really smart tweaks along the way. First and foremost, the 1000XM6s can fold down again. Praise be, which means their carrying case is quite a bit smaller and more compact than last time. And it now uses a magnetic latch closure instead of a zipper. And I honestly love this change. That magnet won't break after wear and tear like a zipper so often does. And the case does stay tightly shut no matter where you kind of pull and tug at it. I'm a little worried about how durable the folding joint on these headphones will be over time, but we'll see. So, what else is better? Well, the headband is thicker and more comfortable, and it's much easier to tell which side of the headphones is the front. Both because of how they're shaped on your head and the fact that there's no seam on the front anymore. And the power button is round now, which means no more mixing it up with the A and C button. By the way, Sony, why were those the same shape for so long? Come on. Oh, and the ear pads are much easier to pull off and replace if need be. You can just yank them off without needing a special tool, a spudger, or a knife. Putting them back on probably takes practice and more time than I had, but this is certainly a change for the better. Now, inside these headphones is Sony's latest processor chip, which is part of why the company claims the ThousandX M6s have the best noise cancellation on the market at their time of release. Bose, they're talking about you. And there's now a total of 12 microphones versus eight last time, which also helps the headphones identify and reduce ambient sound around you. And these mics now use AI beam forming to separate your voice from everything else in the background. Now, I've heard this before. Companies always say their latest headphones are much better at this kind of thing, but we need more extensive realworld tests to know just how much clearer these really are. Then we come to audio quality, and Sony says the ThousandX M6s were tuned by mastering engineers from several of its own music studios. Okay, sure. I only got to try them briefly and they sounded like Sony headphones and that seems to be good enough for millions of people. You still get LDAC for higher quality Bluetooth streaming, but these are mass market noise cancelling headphones. They're not hi-fi cans. They're punchy and they're fun to listen to. Sony gives people what they want. Now, I'm less sure that anyone wants the new spatial audio upmixing feature. These M6s can take stereo audio, be that music or movies, and virtualize it into spatial audio. You're still starting with a left and right channel, but the headphones upmix that into more atmospheric surround sound with headtracking. Now, compared to genuine Dolby Atmos music tracks, Sony is faking it here. Let's be real. And Apple lets you do the same thing with AirPods. Maybe I'll use this when watching Netflix on a plane for that added immersion. Uh, but otherwise, no thanks. Count me out. Some aspects of the ThousandXM6s are unchanged from the M5s. Battery life is the same. 30 hours with&C on and 40 hours supposedly with it off. But I do think there's one new thing that many people, myself included, are going to be pretty excited about. You can finally finally charge and listen to the ThousandXM 6s at the same time. Insert applause here. That said, it's not the way I would have wanted it. See, Sony still isn't supporting wired USBC audio. You can listen through the headphone jack and charge the headphones over USBC at the same time, but that's two cords. And why in the year 2025 can't we just simplify this down to one cable for everything? Apple's doing USBC audio. Sennheiser's doing USBC audio. Bowers and Wilkins USBC audio. I just I don't get it. And now we're going to have to wait until the 1000XM7s, god knows when, for this to be resolved. This doesn't ruin Sony's new headphones for me, but it's certainly a bummer. Usually, I would tell you at this point how much the headphones cost, but at the time of this video, I don't know. Sony didn't share that with us until later on. And so, the M6s cost this much compared to the M5s, which sold for $400 3 years ago. But now, there are tariffs and all kinds of uncertainty in the world. So, this is how things go. Either way, there's a lot to like about the M6s. They're comfortable. They sound promising. The ANC is best in class. If you've got the M5s, there's no reason to rush out and buy these. But if you have the M4s and something earlier, there are a lot of quality of life improvements here that add up to a pretty compelling set of headphones. So, I'm excited and hopefully many of you are as well. Those are just my first impressions of Sony's new ThousandXM6 headphones. But for more headphones and other tech and news, check out the verge.com. And thanks as always for watching.

You can now fold them away, just like what we've been able to do on older models. Both sides fold in like this, allowing for a more compact case. Also, more on that in a bit. The rest of the design tweaks are more subtle. The band features that modern sleek design of the XM5s, but now is a bit wider for a more comfortable fit. The cuffs are a tad bit taller, too, but with the same amount of cushioning. Stitching is only on the backside, so it looks cleaner. The weight trade-off comes at the expense of more premium materials, just like stainless steel and aluminum, just like on the AirPods Max, which is a heavy 386.2 g. The device is plasticky and the finish, unfortunately, a bit of a smudge magnet. I love this dot right here, almost like braille, which tells you, okay, that's the left side. I put it on correctly. Instead of two similarly shaped buttons, the power button is round, while the button to toggle between noise cancellation and ambient mode is like a stick. Because they're two different shapes, it's easy to tell between the two when you're trying to do something. Finally, if you're not familiar, there are no physical buttons for volume control. Instead, the Right Cup has a built-in touch surface. You swipe up and down to control volume. Double tap to press and play or answer or end a call. Swipe forward to skip to the next track and swipe back to go to the previous one. You can also tap and hold to bring up the voice assistant or rest your palm on it to temporarily switch out of noise cancellation mode. Say you want to be able to hear or talk to someone. Sony calls it quick attention mode. In terms of ports, you get USBC for charging, and in case you're wondering, it's for charging only. You can't use it for audio, but you can now charge and use them at the same time. Finally, there's a full-sized 3.5 mm headphone jack. Because you can now fold them again, the carrying case is smaller than its predecessor, but still not as small as the ones on the XM 4s, which I still prefer. But I like its new magnetic lock mechanism. You save so much time by not having to unzip your case every time you want to take them out. The cases, by the way, are color matched to the headphones, which by the way are available in black, blue, and silver, which is a cream white color. Because portability is of utmost importance to me, I probably will still ditch the case and throw them in a drawstring pouch like this and then chuck them into my bag. [Music] The Sony XM 6s are probably the most comfortable noiseancelling headphones I have ever worn. The cushioning is soft and plush, and it embraces my ears quite well. The clamping force is strong enough to provide a light seal, but not too much where it feels like it might give you a light headache. The earups envelop my ears well, and there's enough depth so your ears don't feel squished. In the week that I've been reviewing them, there have been days where I've had them on for several hours, and I didn't feel a sense of relief from suffocation after taking them off. Lastly, when you have them hanging around your neck, the good news is that you can now have the cups facing inward again, the way they should be. While incredibly popular and consistently on top of many reviewers lists, fun fact, the Sony XM 3, XM4, and XM5s all use the same QN1 processor. Why change it if it ain't broke, right? Well, I guess not until you can come up with something better, and looks like the time is ripe for that. This time around, in an effort to improve noise cancellation, Sony is giving the XM6 a new QN3 chip, which they say is seven times faster, which is a must for all the real-time analysis and AI optimizations that these guys do in the background. The chip powers a new adaptive noise cancelling optimizer, which consistently monitors air pressure among other environmental factors, so it can provide the best kind of noise cancellation given your current surroundings. So whether you're 30,000 ft in the air or walking down a windy street, it's constantly adjusting the noise cancellation based on air pressure, ambient noise, and even movement. That's a big leap from the older autoc, which basically just changed modes based on location or routine. They've also added four additional microphones from 8 to 12. And in my tests across both airplane and street noise sounds, the XM6 consistently offered the most satisfying A&C experience compared to the AirPods Max, Bose QC Ultra, and the older XM5. For me, the XM6 and the AirPods Max are probably the best in terms of noise cancellation. Particularly, that's removing hum, fan noise, and high frequency beeps with ease. The only difference is that it does it with a sort of softness that makes the whole world feel like it's turned down but not erased. In contrast, the AirPods Max create a sharper kind of silence. It's clean, it's surgical, sometimes surreal. It's very impressive, don't get me wrong, but sometimes almost emotionally detached. The XM5 and the QC Ultra, while competent, had more inconsistencies. both canceled low-end rumble effectively, but let through more mid and high frequency noise, sometimes even accentuating certain sounds. Overall, I actually prefer the XM6 as it struck the perfect balance, effective noise cancellation without making me feel disconnected from the world around me. Transparency mode was good, too. I love how I can just rest my palms on the right cup to have a conversation with someone. or when I'm out and about, I just rely on adaptive sound control, which will switch modes based on what I'm [Music] doing. Sony says they've added two additional beam forming mics for a total of six. Beam forming is a technique in which mic sensitivity is focused like a beam in a straight line towards a specific direction. In this case, it's towards your mouth. judgement.community_consensus: Sony WH-1000XM6의 언박싱 및 첫인상을 다루는 동영상입니다. 리뷰어는 제품의 프리미엄 품질과 노이즈캔슬링 기능을 긍정적으로 평가하지만, 일부 사용자는 Sony 헤드폰의 최근 품질 저하를 지적하고 있습니다. 댓글에서는 제품의 사운드 품질과 EQ 조정 가능성에 대한 긍정적인 피드백이 있으며, 일부 사용자는 다른 브랜드(예: Sennheiser, Moondrop)를 선호한다고 언급했습니다.
who_should_skip: 최근 Sony 헤드폰 품질 저하를 우려하는 사용자
judgement.best_use_case: 노이즈가 많은 환경에서 사용하거나, 프리미엄 헤드폰의 첫인상을 확인하고 싶은 경우

finalVerdict.one_line_verdict: 소니 WH-1000XM6는 노이즈 캔슬링과 음질, 휴대성에서 뛰어난 성능을 보이지만, 가격과 일부 사용자에게 불편한 점이 있는 제품이다.
finalVerdict.summary: 소니 WH-1000XM6는 이전 모델인 WH-1000XM5에 비해 노이즈 캔슬링 성능, 투명 모드, 사운드 품질, 디자인, 편의 기능에서 개선된 것으로 평가받고 있습니다. 특히, ANC(Active Noise Cancellation) 기능이 탁월하며, 투명 모드가 자연스럽게 작동하여 실생활에서 유용합니다. 또한, 접이식 디자인의 복귀로 휴대성이 향상되었고, 30시간의 배터리 수명을 제공합니다. 그러나 높은 가격, 장시간 착용 시 귀가 습해지는 문제, 터치 패드 조작의 불편함, USB-C 무손실 오디오 미지원, 일부 사용자에겐 답답한 착용감 등이 단점으로 지적되고 있습니다. 사용자들은 이 제품이 여전히 최고 수준의 노이즈 캔슬링 헤드폰임을 인정하지만, 가격 대비 가치에 대한 의견이 분분합니다. 따라서, 노이즈 캔슬링과 음질을 중시하는 사용자에게 적합하며, 예산이 제한되거나 간단한 터치 제어를 원하는 사용자에게는 비추천됩니다.
top_strengths: 디자인 개선 및 접이식 기능 복귀
top_weaknesses: 장시간 착용 시 귀 습기 증가
who_should_buy: 출퇴근이나 항공/철도 여행 시 사용하는 사용자
who_should_skip: USB-C 무손실 오디오를 필요로 하는 사용자
finalVerdict.upgrade_advice: 기존 WH-1000XM4 또는 이하 모델 사용자는 노이즈 캔슬링 성능과 휴대성 향상, 투명 모드 개선 등을 고려해 업그레이드를 고려할 수 있습니다. 그러나 WH-1000XM5 사용자는 개선 폭이 크지 않아 업그레이드 동기가 부족할 수 있습니다.
finalVerdict.buying_advice: 신규 구매자는 노이즈 캔슬링과 음질, 휴대성을 중시하는 경우에 추천됩니다. 특히, 출퇴근이나 항공/철도 여행 시 활용도가 높습니다. 다만, 높은 가격과 장시간 착용 시의 불편함을 고려해 예산과 사용 목적에 맞게 결정하는 것이 좋습니다.
finalVerdict.content_angle: 소니 WH-1000XM6: 노이즈 캔슬링의 정점, 그러나 가격과 불편함은 넘어야 할 산?
